coredata.dc:title: Targeted ginkgo kernel biomass precursor using eco-friendly synthesis of efficient carbon quantum dots for detection of trace nitrite ions and cell imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Qianchun
Tian, Fengling
Zhou, Qin
Zhang, Changbo
Tang, Shan
Jiang, Li
Du, Shuxin
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are useful in medical imaging and detection of toxins in food. In this work, efficient CQDs are prepared from ginkgo kernels by a hydrothermal reaction. The size distribution of the CQDs is both uniform and narrow, with an average particle diameter of 2.7 nm. We realise a quantum yield of 37.8% from our CQDs. Moreover, the CQDs have high selectivity and sensitivity to NO2 −, with the linear ranges of 0.50–50 μmol/L, and its limit of detection is 0.15 μmol/L, this developed method was applied to detect NO2 − in corn sausage, ham sausage, preserved Szechuan pickle, and hot dog, whose concentration of nitrite were further confirmed by high performance liquid chromatograph. Furthermore, the as-prepared CQDs not only have low toxicity and benign biocompatibility, but also display strong blue fluorescence. Therefore, the CQDs can be used for cell imaging of MCF7 cells. Our results indicate that the proposed CQDs based on ginkgo kernel biomass have great potential for cell imaging and NO2 − detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dot
Ginkgo kernel
Nitrite
MCF7 cell
Cell imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Possible mechanism of CQDs detecting NO2 −
Cytotoxicity of CQDs and cell imaging

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustrates the preparation of CQDs from ginkgo kernels for determination of NO2 − and cell imaging.
Size distribution of CQDs (A) TEM images and size distribution (B) HRTEM images (C) The shape of the CQDs is shown in AFM images (D) Height profile of CQDs.
Elemental analysis of CQDs. (A) Full XPS spectrum, (B) C 1s spectrum, (C) N 1s spectrum, and (D) O 1s high-resolution XPS spectrum.
Optical properties of CQDs. (A) Absorption spectrum from the excitation–emission matrix; (B) Absorption spectrum, FL excitation, and emission spectrum of CQDs.

Nitrite detection in CQDs, Nitrite concentration, and FL intensity. (A) Reaction time of CQDs for NO2 −, (B) Specificity of CQDs of NO2 −, (C) Fluorescence atlas of CQDs solutions with NO2 − at disparate concentrations, (D) Linear plot for the concentration of NO2 − and (F0-F)/F0.
Chromatograms of corn sausage (A), ham sausages (B), preserved Szechuan pickle (C), and hot dogs (D) samples. The standard at 0.20 mg/L (a), obtained by HPLC-UV method (b).
Confocal microscope images of MCF7 cells incubated with diversified concentrations of CQDs at different excitation wavelengths.
Detecting NO2 − in corn sausage, ham sausages, preserved Szechuan pickle, and hot dogs samples (n = 3).

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: a The concentrations in parentheses were detected by HPLC-UV.

head.ce:title: Targeted ginkgo kernel biomass precursor using eco-friendly synthesis of efficient carbon quantum dots for detection of trace nitrite ions and cell imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Biology and Chemistry, Key Laboratory for Analytical Science of Food and Environment Pollution of Qian Xi Nan, Xingyi Normal University for Nationalities, Xingyi 562400, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for Analytical Science of Food and Environment Pollution of Qian Xi Nan

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Biology and Chemistry, Key Laboratory for Analytical Science of Food and Environment Pollution of Qian Xi Nan, Xingyi Normal University for Nationalities, Xingyi 562400, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are useful in medical imaging and detection of toxins in food. In this work, efficient CQDs are prepared from ginkgo kernels by a hydrothermal reaction. The size distribution of the CQDs is both uniform and narrow, with an average particle diameter of 2.7 nm. We realise a quantum yield of 37.8% from our CQDs. Moreover, the CQDs have high selectivity and sensitivity to NO2 −, with the linear ranges of 0.50–50 μmol/L, and its limit of detection is 0.15 μmol/L, this developed method was applied to detect NO2 − in corn sausage, ham sausage, preserved Szechuan pickle, and hot dog, whose concentration of nitrite were further confirmed by high performance liquid chromatograph. Furthermore, the as-prepared CQDs not only have low toxicity and benign biocompatibility, but also display strong blue fluorescence. Therefore, the CQDs can be used for cell imaging of MCF7 cells. Our results indicate that the proposed CQDs based on ginkgo kernel biomass have great potential for cell imaging and NO2 − detection.

ce:section.ce:para: CQDs is zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterials, first developed by Xu et al. from single-walled carbon nanotubes in 2004, the particle sizes are generally between 1 and 10 nm [1–4]. CQDs exhibit excellent biological compatibility, rapid response, strong solubility, outstanding optical performance, low cytotoxicity, and remarkable photoluminescence [5–9], which is widely applicable in cell imaging, ion detection, biosensing, and drug delivery [10–15]. CQDs have emerged as important fluorescent nanomaterials, and are highly desirable in several applications. However, CQD precursors are ultra-high carbon content materials crucial in the preparation of CQDs, it is a vital factor affecting the quantum yields, nature provides a limitless source, inexpensive, and sustainable. including the manufacture of orange juice, coffee grounds, corn silk, green tea, waste paper, petroleum coke, ginkgo leave juice, and ginkgo fruits juice [15–23]. However, the high quantum yields is still a vital challenge. CQDs are generally synthesised via two synthesis methods: the ‘top-down’ and ‘bottom-up’ methods. CQDs can be prepared using the ‘bottom-up’ method with controllable size and uniform shape [24]. The hydrothermal treatment method is simple, sustainable, eco-friendly, and a powerful method based on biomass carbon source precursors. Baragau et al. synthesized carbon quantum dots by hydrothermal synthesis of biomass precursors [25]. Jain et al. used waste to prepare high porosity activated carbon by hydrothermal conversion [26]. Zhao et al. synthesized green carbon dots by using biomass hydrothermal carbon [27]. CQDs prepared by hydrothermal methods have been widely applied in cell imaging and ion detection [28], which use biomass precursors.

Nitrite food additives are generally used as food preservatives in cured meat products in the food processing industry. According to the Chinese National Standards (GB 2760-2014), the maximum allowable amount of residue in food is 30 mg/kg. However, excessive consumption of nitrites increases the risk of cancer and hypertension because nitrites absorbed by the human body react with amines to produce the extra-high carcinogenic precursors of N-nitrosamines [29]. Therefore, develop a strategy including good selectivity and high sensitivity for nitrite determination is very necessary. Various nitrite detection methods have been proposed previously, such as chromatography, spectrophotometry, electrochemical methods, capillary electrophoresis, chemiluminescence, and fluorescence [30–35]. Among these methods, the fluorescence has absorbed much attention, owing to its simple operation, outstanding selectivity, high sensitivity, and low cost. Nevertheless, to date, few fluorescent strategies based on biomass CQDs have been applied for nitrite detection.

In this study, efficient CQDs were fabricated using biomass ginkgo kernels as raw materials through a hydrothermal method. The as-prepared CQDs have a high quantum yield of 37.8%, excellent selectivity, and high sensitivity for nitrite determination. Further, a new method was applied for fast and sensitive analysis of nitrites in corn sausage, ham sausage, preserved Szechuan pickle, and hot dog samples with satisfactory recoveries. Moreover, the CQDs exhibited excellent water solubilities, fluorescence stability, lower cytotoxicity, and superior biocompatibility with MCF7 cells. Thus, our CQDs were successfully used to bioimage MCF7 cells.

Ginkgo kernels were purchased from Pizhou (Xuzhou, China) for the experiments. Pure reagents for analysis, including phosphate buffered saline (PBS), fetal bovine serum (FBS), dimethyl sulfoxide, quinine sulfate, hydrochloric acid (weight 36.5%), NaNO2, Fe(NO3)3, glucose (GCOS), tyrosine (TOS), tryptophan (TPP), NaNO3, NaCl, NaI, Na2SO4, Na2CO3, Na2HPO4, CH3COONa·3H2O, citrate, KCl, BaCl2·2H2O, MgCl2, CaCl2, CuCl2, MnCl2, ZnCl2, and FeCl2 were came from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Dulbecco’s modified eagle medium (DMEM) and 3-(4,5-dimethyl-2-thiazolyl)-2,5-diphenyl-2-Htetrazolium bromide (MTT) were purchased from Suo Laibao Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). MCF7 human breast cancer cells were provided by Kejin Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Wuhan, China). Ultra-pure water from Milli-Q (resistivity > 18.2 MΩ cm) was applied to the whole experiment. All reagents were used as received without further purification.

A KQ2200E ultrasonic cleaning machine (Kunshan, China) was used for the full mixing of solutions. The morphology and size about CQDs were determined using Hitachi-F20 TEM (Tokyo, Japan). The FT-IR spectra were measured using a Bruker Nicolet 6700 spectrometer (Karlsruhe, Germany), fluorescence (FL) spectra is recorded using an RF-6000 FL spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was conducted using ESCALAB 250Xi instrument (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Madison, USA). Ultraviolet visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were obtained using a UV-2450 UV−vis spectrophotometer (Shimazu Co., Japan). SONY's DSC W800 and ZF-20D handheld UV lamps were used to capture fluorescent images at excitation wavelength (EX) of 370 nm. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) images were obtained using a Bruker Multimode 8 AFM system (Karlsruhe, Germany), and the KT7-900-434 high-speed centrifuge by Heller International Trading Co., Ltd. (Kenda, Germany) was applied to the centrifugation. The solution about the pH was studied using an S210 pH meter (Mettler Toledo, Germany). And the AL104 electronic balance was came from Mettler Toledo Measurement Instrument Co., Ltd.; Shanghai, China, which was used to test the reagents used. Confocal microscopic images were obtained by scanning a BX53 FL confocal microscope with a confocal laser (Olympus, Japan). The nitrite contents were further verified by an Agilent 1220 high performance liquid chromatograph (HPLC) equipped with Agilent VWD detector (Agilent Scientific, USA). The FL lifetime of CQDs was measured by FLS1000 (EdinburghInstruments, EI).

Six grams of ginkgo kernels were mashed, followed by the addition of 25.00 mL ultra-pure water ultrasonicated for 25 min. And then, transferred the above mixture into a stainless steel autoclave lined with polytetrafluoroethylene and heated for 12 h at 220 °C. When the reaction was complete, the solution was cooled to room temperature without any interference and ultrasonically dispersed for 20 min. The as-prepared solution was placed in a centrifuge tube, centrifuged at 12000 rpm with 10 min, and subsequently further purified with the 0.22-μm pore diameter percolator to remove colloidal particles. Finally, the CQD solution was obtained and stored at 4 °C until further use, as can be show that Fig. 1 exhibited the preparation and application of CQDs.

The selectivity of the probe method was also investigated. The CQD probe system was treated with NO2 − (including I−, Na+, Fe3+, Ac−, Cl−, CO3 2−, NO3 −, SO4 2−, TOS, TPP, GCOS, HPO4 2−, citrate, K+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, and Fe2+) under the same conditions (50 μmol/L, 100 μL). In addition, we evaluated the sensitivity to NO2 −. Multifarious concentrations of NO2 − (100 μL) were added to a mixture of 50 μL hydrochloric acid (0.1 M) and 4.85 mL CQDs solution (pH = 4.0). Then, the mixture was analysed for 60 min using an FL spectrophotometer. The FL response of the CQDs to various concentrations of NO2 − (0.50–50 μmol/L) was analysed, and the linear fitting equation of FL quenching was applied.

Real applications were conducted to estimate the internal dietary risks of NO2 − in humans. Four samples (corn sausage, ham sausage, preserved Szechuan pickle, and hot dog) were purchased from a local supermarket and homogenised, and 1.50 g of each sample was subsequently used. The extraction was performed in 100 μL of saturated borax solution and 4.00 mL of ultra-pure water with an ultrasonic instrument for 5 min. This procedure was repeated twice. Then, the same volume (100 μL) of potassium ferrocyanide and zinc acetate was added to the combined extract solution for shaking. Subsequently, the mixture was heated for 15 min at 80 °C in water baths. Centrifuge the homogenate at 8000 rpm for another 10 min, then the above homogenate further filtered with 0.22-μm filter membrane after cooling down. Then, the total volume was adjusted to 10.00 mL. Eventually, NO2 − in real samples was tested under the same conditions as standard solutions. The detection of NO2 − was useful in the recovery tests after correction by the blank sample. Sample concentration of nitrite, including corn sausage, ham sausage, preserved Szechuan pickle, and hot dog samples, were further confirmed by HPLC, more details are given in the Supporting Information Section 4.

MCF7 cytotoxicity was detected using the standard MTT method. First, MCF7 cells were incubated in DMEM supplied with 10% heat-inactivated FBS for 24 h. Next, these cells were treated with disparate concentrations of CQDs (0.1, 1, 10, 30, 62.5, and 125 μg/mL) for another 24 h. Third, added the MTT solution (20 μL, 5 mg/mL) into the each hole, and cultured these cells under a moist atmosphere at 37 °C for another 1 h. Subsequently, 150 μL dimethyl sulfoxide was added to per well, and shaken about 10 min to dissolve and crystallise. Eventually, the absorbance of each hole was accurately detected by using the microplate reader. To assess the potential application about the CQDs, it is extremely valuable to conduct in vitro imaging experiments. Primarily, MCF7 cells were cultured into the 96-well plates in DMEM, and it contained with 10% FBS under a moist 5% CO2 environment at 37 °C for 48 h [36]. Then, different concentrations (0, 62.5, and 125 g/mL) of the CQDs solution were mixed with polycoagulated amine solution (8 mg/mL) in each well. These were balanced for 30 min at 37 °C, and then added to the cell culture for 24 h. Soon afterwards, the PBS solution (0.01 mol/L, pH = 7.4) was used to wash these descending cells three times. Finally, the coloured cells were viewed using a laser confocal microscope under three types of FL: blue FL (405 nm), green FL (488 nm), and red FL (561 nm).

To obtain optimal CQD performance, some potential influencing factors were explored, including precursor concentration, reaction temperature, and reaction time on the FL spectrum (Fig. S1). As shown in Fig. S1(A), the FL intensity increased from 5 to 15 mg/mL because of the increase in precursor concentration. However, the FL intensity decreased with the continuous increase in the precursor concentration, possibly due to the aggregation of CQDs. Consequently, the optimal precursor concentration was 15 mg/mL. As shown in Fig. S1(B), the FL intensity of the CQDs was the highest at a reaction temperature of 220 °C. Therefore, 220 °C was selected as the best reaction temperature. As shown in Fig. S1(C), CQDs have the highest FL intensity when the reaction time is 12 h, this can be used as the first-rank condition for subsequent experiments.

TEM and AFM images were used to characterise the morphology and size of the CQDs. As can be seen from Fig. 2 A, the CQDs have both benign dispersion performance and identical morphology, and the HRTEM image is clear with a 0.22-mm planar spacing of lattice fringes, corresponding to the (100) facet of graphite [5]. Fig. 2B shows the result obtained using the ImageJ software to estimate the size of single CQDs. The distribution follows a Gaussian curve. According to the indication of statistical data, the average diameter of CQDs is 2.7 nm, and the approximate particle size is about 1.5–3.8 nm. In Fig. 2C and 2D, it is extremely interesting that the height of CQDs particles shown in AFM images is identical to TEM results, which is approximately 2.7 nm. The XRD patterns show the crystal structures of the CQDs. As shown in Fig. S2(A), the interlamellar spacing of the graphite structure is the same as the poignant diffraction peak value at 22.8°; therefore, CQDs can be designated to the degree (002) planes of graphite [37].

The surface functional groups of CQDs were characterised with FT-IR spectra in the frequency from 4000 to 500 cm−1 (Fig. S2(B)). In the FT-IR spectrum, considering the strong vibration frequency of the peak at 3420 cm−1 clearly demonstrates the existence of O–H bonds [38]. Furthermore, the adjustable frequencies of 2900, 1700, and 1625 cm−1 evidenced by the peak characteristics of C–H, C=O, and C=C groups [39–41], respectively. In addition, C–N functional groups are observed with an adjustable frequency of 1427 cm−1 [42]. Moreover, the C–S group of the bending vibration peak appears at 1211 cm−1 [43]. It is very reasonable to speculate that the peak value of 1049 cm−1 corresponds to characteristic vibration assigned to the C–O–C group [43]. The elemental composition and chemical valence states of the CQDs were further verified by XPS. Fig. 3 A indicates the existence of four elements on the CQDs surface, C (71.03%), N (3.14%), O (25.62%), and S (0.21%), whose homologous peaks can be observed at 284.6, 401.7, 532.0, and 168.6 eV, respectively [44]. Moreover, as shown in Fig. 3B, the groups C=C, C–N, C–S, C–O, and O=C–O attributing to five peaks at 284.5, 285.1, 285.3, 286.2, and 289.0 eV, respectively, in the high-resolution C 1s spectrum [42–46]. In addition, the peaks of the N–(C)3 group (399.8 eV) and C–N–H group (400.4 eV) were distinguished in the N 1s spectrum in Fig. 3C [38]. Furthermore, it is obviously display that three peaks assigned respectively to the C=O, O–H, and C–O–C functional groups at 531.7, 532.1, and 532.9 eV, respectively, in the O 1s spectrum (Fig. 3D) [40,47]. The –C=S– and C–S–C groups can be observed in the high-resolution S 2p spectrum, corresponding to binding energies of 163.1 and 168.5 eV, respectively (Fig. S3) [48,49]. This results of the FT-IR spectra are consistent with the above results. These results clearly demonstrate that CQDs have surface hydrophilic groups with benign solubility.

The excitation emission matrix of the as-prepared CQDs is shown in Fig. 4 A. Owing to the diverse EX, the emission wavelength (EM) is principally concentrated in the range of 300–600 nm. In addition, when the EX is 370 nm, the CQDs exhibit the strongest emission intensity at an EM of 455 nm. The optical properties of the CQDs are obtained with UV–vis and FL spectroscopy (Fig. 4B). In UV range (370 nm), the aqueous solution of the CQDs exhibits a strong blue FL. Moreover, a characteristic peak at 300 nm in the UV–vis absorption spectrum was observed, corresponding to the π−π* transition variations of the surface state of the and C=O bond [50,51]. Interestingly, the FL intensity in Fig. S4 increases with increasing pH, stabilising at pH = 3–5. Subsequently, the decrease in the FL intensity may be due to the reduction of the net surface charge and aggregation of CQDs caused by deprotonation. To further explore the optical properties, the FL quantum yield of the prepared CQDs is investigated based on quinine sulfate as a reference. The preeminent FL quantum yield in Fig. S5 is 37.8%, showing potential prospects for the fabrication of sensitive fluorescent probes.

The reaction time between CQDs and NO2 − plays a critical role in efficiently improving the operational productivity and reproducibility. Variations in the reaction time were investigated and ranged from 0 to 80 min. The results showed that the FL intensity decreased during the 0–60 min period, and the reaction time kinetics attained equilibrium at 60 min (Fig. 5 A). Hence, the reaction time of 60 min was regarded as the key point of the subsequent experiments. As shown in Fig. 5B and Figure S6, the FL intensity was quenched by approximately 58% after the addition of NO2 −. The specificity of the prepared CQDs was assessed for NO2 −, the effects of representative ions such as I−, Na+, Fe3+, Ac−, Cl−, CO3 2−, NO3 −, SO4 2−, TOS, TPP, GCOS, HPO4 2−, K+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, and Fe2+, as well as the molecular citrate on FL quenching of CQDs commonly found in food samples chosen as potentially interfering substances in the detection of NO2 −. These results clearly showed that the FL intensity had no significant changes due to the addition of the above ions and biomolecules, and CQDs had an excellent specificity for NO2 −.

The inset in Fig. 5C shows the FL emission of the CQD solution under ultraviolet light at 370 nm before and after the addition of NO2 −. This is strikingly indicate that the addition of NO2 − can decrease the FL emission of CQD solution. In addition, Fig. 5D shows an all-right linear correlation containing (F0–F)/F0 and the concentration of NO2 − using the Stern–Volmer equation:
(1)
F 0
- F
F 0
= 0.0619 C + 0.01041
where F0 is the CQDs and hydrochloric acid solution, and F contains NO2 − in the CQD solution with the measured FL intensity at 370 nm. C is the concentration of NO2 −. Although the concentration of NO2 − increased from 0.50 to 50 μmol/L, the FL intensity decreased. This is obvious from the correlation coefficient (R 2) of 0.999, and the detection limit was calculated to be 0.15 μmol/L (S/N = 3). The proposal method was compared with previous method of CQDs for the measurement of NO2 − [40,52–55]. As listed in Table S1, the probe method showed an excellent performance, including a high sensitivity, wide linear range, and excellent correlation coefficient.

Through the analysis of the NO2 − content in different food samples, the feasibility of the fluorescent probe for the detection of NO2 − in food was assessed. The following results are shown in Table 1 , and the corresponding values of NO2 − in corn sausage, ham sausage, preserved Szechuan pickle, and hot dog samples were 2.97, 2.71, 2.61, and 2.88 μg/g, respectively. The recovery of real samples was evaluated by adding NO2 −. It can be seen from Table 1 that the spiked recoveries of NO2 − in the four samples were between 92.1% and 113%, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) ranged from 1.7% to 5.4%. The results show that it contributes significantly to the selective detection of NO2 − in pragmatic samples using CQD-based probes. In summary, the developed method has excellent potential for detecting NO2 − in complex samples. At the same time, HPLC-UV was used to further verify that the results were consistent with the measurement results (Fig. 6 and Fig. S7), confirming that the established CQDs method is accuracy and it has potential application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanism of CQDs detecting NO2 −
ce:section.ce:para: The quenching mechanism play a vital role in experimental process. For further observing the FL quenching mechanism of CQDs, the FL lifetime of CQDs in the presence of NO2 − was assessed. As shown in Fig. S8(A), the average lifetime of CQDs was 5.72 ns, the average lifetime of CQDs + 50 μmol/L NO2 − was decreased to 3.27 ns. The lifetime is notably changed when NO2 − was added, the result showed that a dynamic quenching might happen. Moreover, as can be seen from Fig. S8(B), the FT-IR spectra of CQDs and CQDs + NO2 − were acquired, after the adding of NO2 −, there are some new peaks, the quenching course might generate new substances, a new peak at 1668 cm−1 is appeared, which may be because NO2 − interacts with the amide group of CQDs, which is a photoinduced electron transfer effect, giving rise to the FL quenching of CQDs [40,52,56].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity of CQDs and cell imaging
ce:section.ce:para: As a potential bio-labelling reagent, the cytotoxicity and biocompatibility of CQDs must be considered. The cytotoxicity of CQDs to MCF7 cells was assessed using the standard MTT assay. MCF7 cells treated with multifarious concentrations of CQDs (0–125 μg/mL) for 24 h maintained a survival rate of more than 82% (Fig. S9), confirming that CQDs have lower cytotoxicity and excellent biocompatibility. Confocal laser scanning microscopy was applied to photograph distribution of CQDs after MCF7 cell culture, which strongly confirmed that CQDs are a perfect fluorescent probe for cell imaging. As shown in Fig. 7 , cells were cultured with CQDs at various concentrations: under irradiation of an excitation wave of 405 nm, blue FL was observed (EM: 400–488 nm), green FL was viewed under irradiation with an EX of 488 nm (EM: 448–508 nm), then red FL was observed at the irradiation with an EX of 561 nm (EM: 498–588 nm). As can be seen from Fig. 7, the green FL images are more legible and specific than the blue and red FL images. Therefore, these results clearly demonstrate that the prepared CQDs can efficiently pass through the cell membrane to stain the cytoplasm, and can be used as an effective FL probe for in vivo imaging.

In summary, efficient CQDs were synthesised by a feasible, green, and facile hydrothermal treatment using ginkgo kernels as raw material, it is gratifying that the biomass precursors without any further treatment. The hydrothermally prepared CQDs exhibited an excellent quantum yield of approximately 37.8%, with fine particles of average diameter 2.7 nm and a narrow distribution. Remarkably, the CQDs displayed good selectivity and outstanding sensitivity to NO2 − in food, and FL quenching occurred under the induction of NO2 −. The concentration of NO2 − can be further verified by HPLC-UV. Moreover, the CQDs displayed excellent biological compatibility, strong water solubility, outstanding optical performance, lower cytotoxicity, and conspicuous photoluminescence. Therefore, our eco-friendly biomass CQDs will be suitable for a wide range of applications in biological imaging.

Qianchun Zhang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Fengling Tian: Investigation, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Qin Zhou: Methodology. Changbo Zhang: Supervision, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Shan Tang: Resources, Writing – review & editing. Li Jiang: . Shuxin Du: Software, Conceptualization, Data curation, Validation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21505115), the Top Scientific and Technological Talents in Universities of Guizhou Province (KY2018078), the Science and Technology for Youth Talent Growth Project of the Guizhou Provincial Education Department (KY2020217), the Key Laboratory for Analytical Science of Food and Environment Pollution of Qian Xi Nan (2021-2-31).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.inoche.2022.109442.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon quantum dots for energy applications: A review

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Advances, challenges and promises of carbon dots

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis and applications of red-emissive carbon dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J.Y. Wang, Y.H. Zhu, L. Wang, Synthesis and applications of red-emissive carbon dots, Chem. Rec. 19 (2019) 2083–2094.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly fluorescent nitrogen and boron doped carbon quantum dots for selective and sensitive detection of Fe3+

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y.H. Zhang, H.X. Qin, Y.T. Huang, F. Zhang, H.R. Liu, H.B. Liu, Z.J. Wang, R. Li, Highly fluorescent nitrogen and boron doped carbon quantum dots for selective and sensitive detection of Fe3+, J. Mater. Chem. B 9 (2021) 4654–4662.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Inherent laws between tetrahedral arrangement pattern and optical performance in tetrahedron-based mid-infrared nonlinear optical materials
